email.subject: Daily Journey: Walker Miles on www.skechers.com

review.raw_markdown:
---

# Walker Miles — Skechers.com Mobile Journey Review
### April 4, 2026 | vs. Yesterday's Score: 4/10

---

## 1. Executive Summary

Look, I'll be straight with you. Some things felt better today — the homepage had my Slip-ins front and center, the men's nav was clean and easy to tap through, and the product page looked sharp. But then I hit the cart and got a completely blank white screen. Nothing. After spending all that time picking out a $135 pair of shoes? That's not just frustrating — that's me closing the app and not coming back. The blank cart is a showstopper, full stop.

---

## 2. Business Impact Score: **4/10**

No change from yesterday. The top of the funnel improved marginally, but a blank cart page is a conversion-killing regression that cancels out any gains. You can't sell shoes if the checkout disappears.

---

## 3. What's Working

**Homepage hero is dead-on.** First thing I saw was a man my age wearing Skechers with the "HANDS FREE Slip-ins" headline big and bold below the fold. That's me. That speaks to me. The BOGO 50% OFF banner was impossible to miss at the very top — as a Skechers Plus member who watches his wallet, that got my attention immediately.

**Men's category nav is genuinely good.** Clean rows, large touch targets, no clutter. "Hands Free Slip-ins" was right there at the bottom of the menu as a quick link — I didn't have to dig through subcategories. "Comfort Technologies" as its own row is a nice touch. Even with my big fingers on a phone screen, this was easy.

**Product page communicates clearly.** The Azusah had a large photo, readable price ($135), deal confirmation ("Buy 1 Get 1 50% off! Applied at Cart"), the Hands Free Slip-ins badge, and star rating all visible without scrolling. Nothing felt cramped.

**ADD TO CART button is excellent.** Pinned to the bottom of the screen, full width, bold blue — hard to miss, easy to tap. That's how it should be done.

**Search finds the right product category.** Typing "slip in" returned 1,017 results and the page loaded fast. The search understood what I was looking for.

---

## 4. What's Weak

**The cart page is completely blank.** Step 8 — pure white. Nothing loaded. After selecting a product, going through sizing, and tapping Add to Cart, I land on an empty page. This is the single most damaging issue on the entire site. For someone like me who isn't going to troubleshoot a broken webpage, this is where I give up and either call the 800 number or just don't buy.

**Login made zero visible difference.** The account icon in the header never changed. No "Hi, Walker" greeting. No "Welcome back, Plus member" acknowledgment. The cart badge stayed at 0 throughout. I have no idea if I was actually logged in. For a loyalty member who's been buying Skechers for years, that's a cold shoulder.

**Size selector boxes are too small.** The grid of sizes (7.0 through 14.0) has boxes that are tight for phone tapping. I accidentally triggered "Please select a size" — the system warned me before I could add to cart, which is actually fine — but hitting the right size box on the first try is harder than it should be.

**Search defaults to Women's results.** I searched "slip in" while supposedly logged in as a male customer. The first two products I saw were both labeled "Women's." If Skechers knows I'm a guy, show me men's shoes first. This feels like the site doesn't know who I am at all.

**The popup rotation was invisible friction.** Between steps 1 and 2, the promotional banner swapped from "Spring Style Guide" to "Shoe Finder" — not a true popup dismiss, just a rotating banner. I didn't have to do anything, but it's worth noting the site is cycling promotional messages that aren't personalized to me as a Slip-ins shopper.

---

## 5. Recommendations

1. **Fix the cart page immediately.** This is Priority Zero. A blank cart is a broken purchase flow. Every day this exists is lost revenue. Test across devices and browsers.

2. **Make logged-in state visible.** Show "Hi, Walker" or "Plus Member" in the header. Confirm my rewards status on login. Even a small indicator that the site knows who I am builds trust.

3. **Filter search by gender when user is logged in.** If I'm a male customer and I search "slip in," show me men's results by default. Give me a toggle to expand to all, but don't start with women's shoes.

4. **Increase size selector touch targets.** Larger boxes, more spacing between them. For phone users with average adult fingers, the current grid is too tight.

5. **Add a "Recommended for You" section.** I'm a Skechers Plus loyalty member. Use that data. Show me Slip-ins I might like based on past purchases — don't make me browse 262 results cold.

---

## 6. Bottom Line

The path from homepage to product detail is honestly pretty good — clean nav, correct product category featured, readable text, deal visible throughout. Skechers is doing right by guys like me in the first half of the funnel. But the cart catastrophe at the end erases all of that goodwill. I found the shoes, I wanted to buy them, and the site broke. That's not a minor UX issue — that's a failed transaction. Until the cart loads reliably, the score stays at 4.

**Score: 4/10** — No change from yesterday. Top-of-funnel marginal improvement, but blank cart is a regression that cannot be overlooked.

---

## 7. Evidence

### Step 1 — Homepage
What I saw: BOGO 50% OFF banner at top. Clean header with search, account, cart icons and MENU. A hero image of a man and woman in casual Skechers, with "HANDS FREE Slip-ins / ARCH FIT" headline below. The man in the photo looked about my age — that resonated.
Easy to use on phone: Yes. Text readable, icons appropriately sized.
Friction: None at this step.
Personalization: None — standard anonymous homepage. No loyalty greeting.

### Step 2 — Dismiss Popups
What I saw: Banner rotated to "Find the Perfect Pair with our Shoe Finder. Get Started." No modal popup to dismiss — the page was otherwise identical to Step 1.
Easy to use on phone: Yes.
Friction: None. The "popup" was just a banner swap, not a true interstitial.
Personalization: None.

### Step 3 — Log In
What I saw: Returned to homepage with "Spring Style Guide! Shop Now" banner. Cart still showed "0." Account icon unchanged — no visible confirmation of being logged in.
Easy to use on phone: Unknown — the login screen itself wasn't captured; only the return to homepage was visible.
Friction: High uncertainty. No visual indicator that login succeeded. I genuinely wouldn't know if I was logged in.
Personalization: None visible post-login.

### Step 4 — Men's Category
What I saw: A clean drawer-style menu. MEN header at top. Rows for Shoes, Shop by Activity, Comfort Technologies, Collections, Collaborations, Clothing & Accessories — each with a right-arrow. Below the divider: Hands Free Slip-ins (bold, tappable), Wide Fit, Extra Wide Fit.
Easy to use on phone: Very easy. Large touch targets, clear labels, logical grouping.
Friction: None. This is one of the best-designed screens in the journey.
Personalization: "Hands Free Slip-ins" as a quick link is relevant to me, though it's there for everyone.

### Step 5 — Men's Shoes (Hands Free Slip-ins)
What I saw: "Hands Free Slip-ins" as a large page heading. Descriptive text about Heel Pillow technology. Width filter buttons (Wide Fit, Extra Wide Fit, Medium — third partially cut off). "262 Results." Filter and Sort by. Products just starting to appear at the very bottom.
Easy to use on phone: Mostly yes. The filter buttons are well-sized. 262 results is a manageable number.
Friction: Third width filter button cut off at screen edge — I might not know there's a "Medium" or "Standard" option.
Personalization: Width options are convenient but not personalized to my account profile.

### Step 6 — View Product
What I saw: Men's breadcrumb. Full product title. 4-star rating, 8 reviews. $135.00 price. "Buy 1 Get 1 50% off! Applied at Cart" confirmation in red/orange. Hands Free Slip-ins badge. Large product photo of a white/gray/blue shoe with nav arrows.
Easy to use on phone: Yes. Well laid out, nothing crowded.
Friction: None. This is a strong product page.
Personalization: The BOGO deal applied feels like a benefit — even if it's sitewide and not loyalty-specific.

### Step 7 — Add to Cart
What I saw: Size grid (7.0–14.0 in half sizes), three rows. "Please select a size" warning. QTY dropdown at 1. Ship it / Check in-store availability options. Full-width blue "ADD TO CART" button pinned to bottom.
Easy to use on phone: Mostly. The ADD TO CART button is excellent. The size boxes are small.
Friction: Hit the "please select size" validation — understandable, but the size boxes could be bigger to make selecting easier on a phone.
Personalization: No saved size from my account on file, no pre-population.

### Step 8 — View Cart
What I saw: A completely blank, white screen. Nothing. No products, no totals, no navigation, no error message. Just white.
Easy to use on phone: It did not function at all.
Friction: Maximum. This is a broken page and a failed purchase.
Personalization: Irrelevant — the page didn't load.

### Step 9 — Search ("slip in")
What I saw: "SLIP IN" as the search heading. 1,017 results. Filter and Sort by controls. First two visible products: Skechers Slip-ins Arch Fit Arcade (Women's, Also in Wide) and Skechers Slip-ins Contour Foam Cozy Fit (Women's, Also in Wide). Both clearly labeled Women's.
Easy to use on phone: Yes — the results page layout is clean, product cards are readable.
Friction: As a male shopper, seeing Women's products first is disorienting and irrelevant. I'd likely hit Filter and try to sort it out — extra steps I shouldn't need.
Personalization: Poor. A logged-in male customer should see men's results defaulted.

---
*Walker Miles | 62 | Skechers Plus Member | Mobile (Phone) Shopper*

---

## Technical Audit

## Technical Summary

Site runs on **Salesforce Commerce Cloud (Demandware)** — confirmed by `/on/demandware.static/Sites-USSkechers-Site/` asset paths. Bot detection via **KapSecurity SDK** (`x-kpsdk-v=j-1.2.308`). Consent management via **TrustArc** with a non-standard `vice-managed-src` attribute pattern used to defer third-party script `src` loading until consent. 20+ third-party tracking scripts load on every page. TTFB is healthy across most steps; cart endpoint is an outlier. LCP and CLS are unmeasured — a gap in the data.

---

## Accessibility

**32 axe violations total. Severity breakdown:**

| Severity | Count | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| Critical | 4 | `aria-required-parent` (Homepage), `aria-required-children`, `aria-valid-attr-value`, `button-name` (View Product) |
| Serious | 5+ | `link-name` (Homepage), `aria-dialog-name`, `aria-input-field-name`, `color-contrast` (View Product), `link-in-text-block` (Men's Shoes) |
| Moderate | 10+ | Duplicate `<main>` landmark, `<aside>` not top-level, incorrect heading order, content outside landmarks |
| Minor | 2 | `aria-allowed-role` on Homepage and Men's Shoes |

**Specific issues:**
- **`button-name` (critical, View Product):** Buttons with no accessible text — completely unusable for screen readers, likely icon-only buttons (e.g., wishlist, close, quantity controls) missing `aria-label`.
- **`aria-valid-attr-value` (critical, View Product):** Invalid ARIA attribute values likely on the size/color selector widgets.
- **`aria-required-parent` / `aria-required-children`:** ARIA list or grid roles with broken parent-child structure — common in custom carousels or product grids.
- **`landmark-no-duplicate-main` (moderate, Homepage):** More than one `<main>` in the DOM. Likely a hidden template or GTM-injected fragment not cleaned up.
- **`link-in-text-block` (serious, Men's Shoes):** Links in body text differentiated only by color — fails WCAG 1.4.1 without underline or other non-color indicator.
- **`aria-dialog-name` (serious, View Product):** Modal dialogs (size guide, quickview) lack `aria-label` or `aria-labelledby`.

---

## SEO

- `<html lang="en">` is present and correct.
- Log In step is recorded at the same URL as Homepage (`https://www.skechers.com/`) — login is a modal, not a separate page. No SEO issue, but confirm the modal doesn't ship with indexable content that shouldn't be indexed.
- `vice-managed-src` is a consent-management attribute pattern — third-party scripts with this attribute won't fire without consent, but Googlebot won't consent, so some tag manager destinations may not fire during crawls. Acceptable, but be aware personalization signals won't function for the crawler.
- No `dns-prefetch` or `preconnect` for critical first-party asset origins visible in source — only Monetate is hinted.
- Heavy synchronous-rendered HTML from SFCC is crawler-friendly; no client-side-only routing observed.

---

## Performance

| Step | TTFB | LCP | CLS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Homepage | 33ms | — | — |
| Log In | 53ms | — | — |
| Men's Category | 53ms | — | — |
| Men's Shoes | 29ms | — | — |
| View Product | 35ms | — | — |
| Add to Cart | 35ms | — | — |
| **View Cart** | **134ms** | — | — |
| Search | 32ms | — | — |

- **TTFB:** All steps within acceptable range except **View Cart at 134ms** — 4x higher than the next-slowest step. Cart likely involves server-side session/inventory lookup. Investigate SFCC cart controller performance.
- **LCP and CLS not captured** — critical Web Vitals gap. LCP is especially important on image-heavy category and PDP pages.
- **Script deduplication failure:** `tags.creativecdn.com/dhGobFICKSkk6J9nvQPw.js` loaded **twice** on Homepage and Men's Category. `s.yimg.com/wi/ytc.js` loaded **twice** on multiple pages. These are likely GTM tag configuration errors causing duplicate pixel fires and wasted bandwidth.
- **20+ async third-party scripts in `<head>`:** Even with `async`, this many concurrent connections on mobile networks will contend for bandwidth and delay LCP. No `preconnect` hints for heavy origins (TikTok, Facebook, Quantum Metric, Bloomreach).
- **WebFontLoader** loaded from Cloudflare CDN (`cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/webfont/1.6.28/webfontloader.js`) — this is an external dependency for font loading; if it's slow or fails, FOUT/FOIT will occur.

---

## Mobile Optimization

- **`--vh: 6.640000000000001px`** is set as an inline style on `<html>`. This is a JavaScript workaround for iOS Safari's dynamic toolbar changing `100vh`. The floating-point artifact (`6.640000000000001` instead of a clean value) is a JS precision issue — functionally harmless but indicates the value is being calculated directly without rounding (e.g., `window.innerHeight * 0.01` without `toFixed`). Fix: `document.documentElement.style.setProperty('--vh', (window.innerHeight * 0.01).toFixed(3) + 'px')`.
- `wf-mulish-*` classes on `<html>` confirm WebFontLoader is controlling Mulish font loading state — FOUT flash will occur on slow connections until these classes apply.
- No evidence of `<meta name="viewport">` in the truncated source, but its absence would be catastrophic — assume it's present further in `<head>`.

---

## Console & Network Errors

**429 Rate Limiting (KapSecurity fingerprinting):**
- Endpoint: `/149e9513-01fa-4fb0-aad4-566afd725d1b/2d206a39-8ed7-437e-a3be-862e0f06eea3/fp?x-kpsdk-v=j-1.2.308`
- **5 consecutive 429s** on the same endpoint. KapSecurity's bot-detection SDK is being rate-limited — likely expected during automated audits, but the client retries without backoff, spamming the endpoint. Real users could hit this if the rate limit is set too aggressively.
- **`/cart/` returns 429** — this is a user-facing page returning rate-limit errors. If triggered in production for real users (e.g., during high traffic or rapid cart operations), it would break checkout entry. Investigate whether the cart controller shares rate limit quota with the fingerprinting endpoint.

**CORS failure (Stylitics):**
- `https://web-assets.stylitics.com/style.css?base=hotspots&version=320dd7a42c` is blocked by CORS: `No 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin'` header.
- This blocks the Stylitics "shop the look" / hotspot widget from rendering its styles. The request is being made via both `<link>` (CSS) and `XMLHttpRequest` — the XHR attempt is a fallback that also fails. Stylitics is a vendor-side misconfiguration; Skechers should open a ticket with Stylitics to add `https://www.skechers.com` to their CORS allowlist, or proxy the asset through a Skechers-controlled CDN origin.

---

## Recommendations

1. **[High] Fix Stylitics CORS** — `web-assets.stylitics.com` must include `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://www.skechers.com`. Escalate to Stylitics, or proxy the stylesheet through a Skechers CDN edge.

2. **[High] Investigate `/cart/` 429** — Determine if the cart rate limit shares a bucket with KapSecurity fingerprinting. A real user hitting 429 on `/cart/` cannot proceed to checkout.

3. **[High] Fix 4 critical ARIA violations on View Product** — `button-name`, `aria-valid-attr-value`, `aria-required-children` are blocking for screen reader users on the most conversion-critical page.

4. **[High] Measure LCP and CLS** — All 8 steps are missing these metrics. LCP on PDP and category pages is almost certainly the dominant Web Vitals issue given the image density.

5. **[Medium] Deduplicate GTM-fired scripts** — `tags.creativecdn.com` and `s.yimg.com/wi/ytc.js` fire twice per page. Audit GTM container for duplicate tags; this wastes bandwidth and may double-count analytics/pixel events.

6. **[Medium] Add KapSecurity retry backoff** — The SDK fires 5+ retries instantly on 429. Implement exponential backoff with jitter to reduce request volume under rate pressure.

7. **[Medium] Fix `--vh` floating-point precision** — Round to 3 decimal places to avoid precision artifacts propagating into CSS `calc()` expressions.

8. **[Low] Add `preconnect` for critical third-party origins** — Replace or supplement `dns-prefetch` with `<link rel="preconnect">` for Bloomreach (`cdns.brsrvr.com`), Quantum Metric (`cdn.quantummetric.com`), and TikTok pixel origins. This reduces connection setup latency for scripts that fire early.

9. **[Low] Audit `aria-dialog-name` on modals** — Size guide, quickview, and cart drawer modals on View Product lack accessible names. Add `aria-labelledby` pointing to the modal heading.
